failure_modes.text: WYSIATI can make the buffer look sufficient because the team never surfaces the failure evidence that would widen it.
failure_modes.source_quote: What You See Is All There Is (WYSIATI)
failure_modes.mitigation: Run a pre-mortem specifically for hidden failure evidence: ask what the team would need to believe for the buffer to be too small, then check whether anyone holds that evidence.

failure_modes.text: Overconfidence can shrink the needed margin because the operator assumes they know enough without further investigation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate the margin against historical miss rates, not against current confidence: use reference-class data from past projects to size the buffer.

failure_modes.text: Conjunctive-events bias can make timelines or budgets look safe even though success depends on too many steps going right.
failure_modes.source_quote: People tend to overestimate the overall probability of these conjunctive events, leading to unwarranted optimism about a plan succeeding or being completed on time.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose the plan into independent steps and check how many must succeed simultaneously; widen the margin when conjunctive dependencies stack up.

premortem_questions.text: A margin of safety is only real if it is wider than your recent track record of estimation errors. Before committing: what was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates this team made?
premortem_questions.source_quote: A margin of safety is only real if it is wider than your recent track record of estimation errors -- not wider than your best-case model. Before committing: what was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates this team made?

premortem_questions.text: What key facts or inputs have we ignored because they didn't fit our narrative?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What key facts or inputs have we ignored because they didn't fit our narrative?

premortem_questions.text: What would we have to believe for the opposite hypothesis to be true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would we have to believe for the opposite hypothesis to be true?

heuristics.text: Define failure first and devote effort to preventing it instead of describing only success.
Map known knowns, known unknowns, and unknown unknowns before deciding how much buffer the system needs.
Model the full distribution of outcomes and ask whether the worst case is bad enough before proceeding.
heuristics.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

curation_notes.summary: The raw source is strong on downside-protection failure patterns and pre-mortem discipline, so the chunk set stays close to those interventions.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor finance-specific implementation examples because the raw playbook already gives stronger structural mitigation content.
open_questions: The first premortem question is trimmed slightly for chunk usability, but it remains directly grounded in the raw mitigation table.

allies: The raw allies section is useful later relation material around probabilistic thinking and structured hypothesis challenge.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is thinner than the structured tensions and also points to optimism and availability-style distortions that do not map cleanly to current ids.
structured_tensions: The raw source explicitly preserves tensions against optionality, feedback-loops, and confirmation-bias.
